content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorvIMIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
March31, 1998
Richard Radan
7418 Midiron Drive
Fair Oaks, California 95628
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-076
Dear Mr. Radan:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-government
employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ We provided advice to you in
the Radan Advice Letter, No. 1-98-041 regarding similar issues. You have requested further
assistance.
Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed) provides that the Commission may decline to provide
informal assistance or may limit such assistance to the explanation, in general terms, of the
requirements of the Act if assistance or advice is being sought regarding the duties of another
person and the requestor does not appear to be authorized to make the request as the person’s
representative. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(4)(C).) Accordingly, the advice in this letter will be
limited to a general explanation ofthe Act as it pertains to your facts.
2
QUESTION
You are a designated employee of a state agency. If you went to work for an employer in
the private sector, would that employer be allowed to bid on a contract implementing part of an
automated welfare system if you did not participate in the bidding process?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prevent a prospective employer from bidding on a project involving a
designated employee’s former government agency. However, the designated employee may not
be identified in connection with the contract.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity conferred by formal written
advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-98-076
Page 2
FACTS
You work at the Health & Welfare Data Center (HWDC) as a deputy director (CEA II)
managing several large information technology projects. One of these projects is the Statewide
Automated Welfare System (SAWS). The welfare program in California is supervised by the
state, and administered and operated by the counties. The state’s role in the SAWS project is one
of oversight.
Counties have organized into four consortia and are responsible for planning,
procurement, development, implementation and operation of their automated welfare systems.
These will be county procurements where the counties will develop and evaluate the request for
proposals and select the winning vendor. The contract will be between the county and the
vendor. The state will approve the funding for the project and monitor the progress and budget.
ANALYSIS
The Act places three restrictions on the activities of designated employees of state
agencies who are leaving state service. Two are post-employment, while the third regulates
activities ofthe official before leaving.
Section 87406 prohibits designated employees, for one year after leaving state service,
from being paid to communicate with their former agency in an attempt to influence legislative
or administrative action or any action or proceeding involving a permit, license, contract or
transaction involving property or goods.
Sections 87400-87405 prohibit designated employees from being paid to appear before
their former agency or a court in a proceeding involving specific events (e.g., a lawsuit,
administrative hearing, or a state contract) in which the employee participated.
Section 87407 prohibits designated employees from making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision that directly relates to a prospective employer.
These prohibitions do not prevent a prospective employer from bidding on a project
involving a designated employee’s former government agency.
3
Please note that section 83116.5 provides that any person who aids and abets or causes another person to
violate the Act has also violated the Act. Before the passage of Proposition 208, section 83116.5 was limited to
persons who have filing obligations under the Act or who were compensated for services involving the planning,
organizing, or directing of activity regulated by the Act. This limitation was repealed by Proposition 208.
Proposition 208 is currently subject to a preliminary injunction. (California Prolfe Council Political Action
Committee v. Scully (E.D. Cal. 1998) ---- F.Supp. ----, No. CIV. S-96-l965 LICK/DAD, 1998 WL 7173.)


File No. 1-98-076
Page 3
I have enclosed additional information for further guidance. If you have any other
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

QUESTION: You are a designated employee of a state agency. If you went to work for an employer in
the private sector, would that employer be allowed to bid on a contract implementing part of an
automated welfare system if you did not participate in the bidding process?

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prevent a prospective employer from bidding on a project involving a
designated employee’s former government agency. However, the designated employee may not
be identified in connection with the contract.2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity conferred by formal written
advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
